source: BluPrint Magazine, PHILGBC, UNITED Architects of the Philippines, Industry Practice 2024-2026
notes: These are observed design trends, not code requirements. They reflect current market demand, award-winning projects, and emerging best practices in Philippine architecture.

rules.description: Elevated living platform on stilts — modern interpretation of bahay kubo
rules.notes: Reinterpreting the traditional elevated house using concrete/steel stilts. Ground level remains open for parking, gardens, or flood resilience. Trending in Batangas, Palawan, Siargao resort architecture.

rules.description: Steep pitched roof with exposed timber or steel trusses
rules.notes: Cathedral ceilings with visible structure celebrate Filipino craftsmanship. Pitch 30-45° for rain shedding and thermal stack ventilation. Trending material: engineered bamboo laminate trusses.

rules.description: Modern capiz, solihiya, or banig-inspired screen walls
rules.notes: Traditional weaving patterns (solihiya cane, banig mat) translated into CNC-cut metal, perforated concrete, or woven rattan panels. Provides privacy, ventilation, and cultural identity. Kenneth Cobonpue's influence.

rules.description: Open-plan living merging indoor and outdoor via full-width sliding doors
rules.notes: Multi-panel folding glass doors (4-8 panels) that open entire wall to garden/terrace. Creates the traditional Filipino kamalig flow where interior breathes into the landscape.

rules.description: Local stone and wood as feature materials against clean white/concrete volumes
rules.notes: Coral stone (Mactan), adobe (Pampanga), Romblon marble, or reclaimed molave as accent walls/floors against minimalist concrete. Celebrating provenance — material tells a regional story.

rules.description: Exposed board-formed concrete (fair-face) with tropical planting
rules.notes: Raw concrete walls softened by cascading plants, vertical gardens, and weathered patina. The tropical climate ages concrete beautifully. Inspired by Geoffrey Bawa (Sri Lanka) and Brazilian modernism, adapted to PH.

rules.description: Perforated concrete block (breeze block / ventilation block) as facade system
rules.notes: Geometric patterned CHB (concrete hollow blocks) used as ventilated screens. Provides privacy, airflow, filtered light, and visual texture. Revived from 1960s-70s Filipino modernism. Low cost, locally made.

rules.description: Cantilevered concrete volumes creating deep shade and rain protection
rules.notes: 2-3m cantilevers over outdoor spaces, eliminating the need for separate awnings. Bold structural gesture that also functions as passive cooling. Post-tensioned slabs enable longer spans.

rules.description: Interior courtyard (silong/looban) as central organizing element
rules.notes: Courtyard houses are traditional in Vigan and ancestral homes. Modern version: double-height planted atriums, internal gardens, or light wells that bring nature into the building core.

rules.description: Living green walls on building facades — vertical forest trend
rules.notes: Manila condo and office towers increasingly feature planted facades. Reduces OTTV, improves air quality, and softens urban edges. SHDA and Ayala Land projects pioneering this.

rules.description: Rooftop urban farms and productive landscapes on commercial buildings
rules.notes: Beyond ornamental green roofs — actual food production. Trending in BGC, Makati, and Cebu IT Park developments. Connects to RA 11511 (Urban Agriculture). Some buildings grow 30% of cafeteria produce.

rules.description: Water features as thermal comfort and sensory design elements
rules.notes: Reflecting pools, rain chains replacing downspouts, infinity edges, and shallow wading courts. Evaporative cooling + ambient sound. Filipino love of water (tubig) made architectural.
